content: What is the equivalent of sharp package that I can use in Rust with Tauri?

If you’re coming from **Sharp (Node.js)**, the key thing to understand is:

> Sharp = a high-level API built on top of **libvips** (a very fast C image processing library) [insights.linuxfoundation.org+1](https://insights.linuxfoundation.org/collection/image-processing-libraries?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Rust doesn’t have a _single dominant “Sharp-equivalent”_, but you’ve got a few solid options depending on how close you want to get to Sharp’s capabilities.

* * *

# 🧠 Closest “Sharp-like” options in Rust

## 1\. 🥇 `photon-rs` (best high-level equivalent)

-   Probably the **closest experience to Sharp**
    
-   High-level API: resize, crop, filters, watermark, etc.
    
-   Works **natively + WebAssembly (great for Tauri)**
    

**Why it feels like Sharp:**

-   Simple API
    
-   Lots of built-in effects
    
-   Fast and safe
    

👉 Key capabilities:

-   Resize, crop, rotate
    
-   Filters & color adjustments
    
-   Blurs, edge detection
    
-   Watermarking & blending [Docs.rs](https://docs.rs/photon-rs/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
    

👉 Performance:

-   Designed for high performance, near-native speed even in WASM [GitHub](https://github.com/silvia-odwyer/photon?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
    

**Verdict:**  
➡️ If you want a **drop-in “Sharp-like” developer experience**, use this.

* * *

## 2\. 🧰 `image` crate (the “standard library” of Rust images)

-   The most widely used Rust image crate
    
-   Lower-level than Sharp
    

👉 Features:

-   Load/save images
    
-   Resize, crop, basic transforms
    
-   Pixel-level access
    

👉 Downsides:

-   No fancy pipeline like Sharp
    
-   You compose things manually
    

**Verdict:**  
➡️ Great foundation, but not a Sharp replacement by itself.

* * *

## 3\. ⚡ `imageflow` (closest to libvips-style pipelines)

-   Designed for **high-performance pipelines**
    
-   More “backend engine” than friendly API
    

👉 From Rust community discussions:

-   Considered a “one-stop shop” for image processing [The Rust Programming Language Forum](https://users.rust-lang.org/t/image-resize-library-with-fit-cover/44845?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
    

**Verdict:**  
➡️ Closer to Sharp’s _architecture_, but less ergonomic.

* * *

## 4\. 🔬 `opencv` (Rust bindings)

-   Very powerful, but heavy
    

👉 Use when:

-   You need advanced processing (CV, detection, etc.)
    

**Verdict:**  
➡️ Overkill if you just want Sharp-like resizing/transform.

* * *

## 5\. 🧪 Others worth mentioning

-   `imageproc` → algorithms on top of `image`
    
-   `resvg` → SVG rendering
    
-   `libvips` bindings (less common, but possible)
    

* * *

# ⚖️ Quick comparison

| Feature | Sharp (Node) | photon-rs | image | imageflow |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| High-level API | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ⚠️ |
| Performance | 🔥 (libvips) | 🔥 | 👍 | 🔥 |
| Pipeline chaining | ✅ | ⚠️ | ❌ | ✅ |
| WASM / Tauri ready | ⚠️ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ |
| Ease of use | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ | ❌ |

* * *

# 🧭 Recommendation for Tauri

For a **Tauri app**, I’d strongly suggest:

### 👉 Start with `photon-rs`

-   Easy to integrate
    
-   Works well with frontend (WASM optional)
    
-   Covers 90% of Sharp use cases
    

* * *

# 💡 If you want something _really_ like Sharp…

There isn’t a perfect 1:1 yet. Sharp is special because:

-   It wraps **libvips**
    
-   It has a very polished API
    

In Rust, you'd typically:

-   Use `photon-rs` for convenience  
    **or**
    
-   Build your own pipeline using `image` + other crates
    

* * *

If you want, I can show you:

-   A **Sharp → Rust (photon/image)** code translation
    
-   Or how to wire this cleanly into a **Tauri command** setup
what is the rust equivalent to ffpmeg ?

The Rust ecosystem actually has a **very clear answer here**, unlike Sharp:

> ✅ The “FFmpeg equivalent” in Rust is still… **FFmpeg itself**, but accessed through Rust bindings.

There’s no fully native Rust library that replaces FFmpeg’s insane feature set yet (encoding, decoding, filters, streaming, codecs, etc.). So in practice, you choose between **bindings** or **wrappers**.

* * *

# 🥇 Best options in Rust

## 1\. `ffmpeg-next` (most direct equivalent)

-   Rust bindings to the real **FFmpeg**
    
-   Low-level, but **closest to using FFmpeg directly**
    

👉 What you get:

-   Full access to codecs, muxing, demuxing
    
-   Frame-by-frame processing
    
-   Works like FFmpeg C API (just in Rust)
    

👉 Downsides:

-   Not beginner-friendly
    
-   Steep learning curve
    

**Verdict:**  
➡️ Use this if you want **maximum control (like raw FFmpeg CLI but programmable)**

* * *

## 2\. `ffmpeg-cli` / calling FFmpeg directly (most common in Tauri)

-   Just spawn the FFmpeg binary from Rust
    

```rust
use std::process::Command;

Command::new("ffmpeg")
    .args([
        "-i", "input.mp4",
        "-vf", "scale=1280:720",
        "output.mp4"
    ])
    .output()?;
```

👉 Why this is popular:

-   You already know FFmpeg commands
    
-   No bindings complexity
    
-   Works perfectly in **Tauri desktop apps**
    

**Verdict:**  
➡️ This is honestly the **most practical “Sharp-style” workflow**

* * *

## 3\. `gstreamer-rs` (alternative ecosystem)

-   Rust bindings for **GStreamer**
    

👉 Pros:

-   Pipeline-based (similar concept to FFmpeg filters)
    
-   Good for streaming apps
    

👉 Cons:

-   Different ecosystem
    
-   Heavier setup
    

**Verdict:**  
➡️ Use if you’re building **real-time media pipelines**, not just file processing

* * *

## 4\. `rav1e`, `x264`, etc. (codec-specific crates)

-   Pure Rust or bindings for specific codecs
    

👉 Examples:

-   AV1 → `rav1e`
    
-   H264 → via bindings
    

👉 Limitation:

-   Not a full FFmpeg replacement
    

**Verdict:**  
➡️ Only useful if you need **one codec**, not a full pipeline

* * *

# ⚖️ Quick comparison

| Option | Level | Ease | Power | Use case |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `ffmpeg-next` | Low-level | ❌ | 🔥🔥🔥 | Full control |
| FFmpeg CLI | High-level | ✅ | 🔥🔥🔥 | Most apps |
| `gstreamer-rs` | Medium | ⚠️ | 🔥🔥 | Streaming |
| Codec crates | Low-level | ⚠️ | 🔥 | Specific codecs |

* * *

# 🧭 Recommendation for Tauri

For your setup (Next.js + Tauri + likely similar mindset to Sharp):

### 👉 Best approach:

**Use FFmpeg CLI via Rust (`Command`)**

Why:

-   Same mental model as Sharp pipelines
    
-   No binding headaches
    
-   Easy to debug
    
-   Cross-platform with Tauri bundling
    

* * *

# 💡 Mental mapping (Sharp → Rust FFmpeg)

-   **Sharp (Node)** → high-level wrapper over libvips
    
-   **Rust equivalent** → no single wrapper → use:
    
    -   `photon-rs` (images)
        
    -   **FFmpeg CLI (video/audio)**
        

* * *

# 🚀 If you want

I can show you:

-   A clean **Tauri command wrapper for FFmpeg**
    
-   How to bundle FFmpeg with your app (important for production)
    
-   Or a **TypeScript → Rust media pipeline equivalent**
When converting from HEIC to PNG what average time would that be? File is 4MB

Short answer: **it’s usually very fast — well under a second** for a 4 MB HEIC file.

* * *

# ⏱️ Typical conversion time (HEIC → PNG)

For a **~4 MB HEIC image** (e.g. iPhone photo, ~12MP):

-   ⚡ **Fast machine (modern desktop / M1 / Ryzen):**  
    → **50–200 ms**
    
-   💻 **Average laptop:**  
    → **100–400 ms**
    
-   🐢 **Low-end / older CPU:**  
    → **300–800 ms**
    

👉 In most real apps (including Tauri), users perceive this as **instant**.

* * *

# 📦 What actually affects the time

The _file size (4MB)_ matters less than the **image resolution + decoding complexity**:

### 1\. 📸 Resolution (biggest factor)

-   12MP (4032×3024) → typical iPhone → fast
    
-   48MP (newer iPhones) → can be **2–4× slower**
    

* * *

### 2\. 🧠 HEIC decoding cost

-   HEIC uses HEVC compression (complex)
    
-   Decoding is the **slowest step**, not PNG encoding
    

* * *

### 3\. ⚙️ Library used

Different tools can vary a lot:

-   **FFmpeg** → fast, optimized
    
-   **libheif (via bindings)** → also very fast
    
-   Pure JS/WASM → slower (can hit 1–2s)
    

* * *

### 4\. 🧵 Threading

-   FFmpeg can use multiple threads → faster
    
-   Some Rust crates are single-threaded → slower
    

* * *

# 🔥 Real-world expectation in Tauri

If you:

-   Use **FFmpeg CLI** OR `libheif` bindings
    
-   Run conversion in a Rust command (not blocking UI)
    

👉 You’ll typically get:

> ✅ **~100–300 ms per image**

* * *

# ⚠️ When it gets slower

You might see **\>1s** if:

-   Very high resolution (e.g. 48MP HEIC)
    
-   Running on low-end hardware
    
-   Using WASM or JS-based decoders
    
-   Doing batch conversions without concurrency
    

* * *

# 💡 Practical advice

-   Treat it as **“instant but async”**
    
-   Always run in a background thread in Tauri
    
-   Show a spinner only if >300ms
    

* * *

# 🧪 Rule of thumb

> 📏 **~0.1–0.3 seconds per standard HEIC photo**

* * *

So can ffmpeg convert HEIC to png?

Yes — **FFmpeg can convert HEIC to PNG**, and it’s actually one of the most reliable ways to do it.

* * *

# ✅ Basic command

```bash
ffmpeg -i input.heic output.png
```

That’s it — FFmpeg will:

-   Decode the HEIC image
    
-   Encode it as PNG
    

* * *

# ⚙️ Things to know

### 1\. 🧩 HEIC support depends on build

FFmpeg needs HEIC/HEIF support compiled in (via **libheif**).

👉 Check with:

```bash
ffmpeg -codecs | grep heic
```

If you don’t see HEIC/HEIF:

-   Your FFmpeg build doesn’t support it
    
-   You’ll need a different build (common on Linux)
    

* * *

### 2\. 🖼️ Multiple images (Live Photos / bursts)

HEIC files can contain multiple frames.

To extract the first frame:

```bash
ffmpeg -i input.heic -frames:v 1 output.png
```

* * *

### 3\. 🎯 Preserve quality (PNG is lossless anyway)

PNG is lossless, but you can control compression:

```bash
ffmpeg -i input.heic -compression_level 100 output.png
```

(Default is usually fine.)

* * *

### 4\. 📐 Resize during conversion (like Sharp)

```bash
ffmpeg -i input.heic -vf scale=1024:-1 output.png
```

* * *

# ⚡ Performance

As mentioned earlier:

-   Typically **~100–300 ms** for a standard photo
    
-   FFmpeg is highly optimized (multi-threaded)
    

* * *

# 🧠 When FFmpeg is a great choice

Use FFmpeg if you want:

-   One tool for **images + video + audio**
    
-   Easy CLI integration in **Tauri**
    
-   Reliable HEIC decoding
    

* * *

# ⚠️ When NOT to use FFmpeg

Consider `libheif` directly if:

-   You only process images
    
-   You want a smaller binary footprint
    
-   You don’t need video/audio features
    

* * *

# 🧭 For your Tauri app

Best pattern:

-   Bundle FFmpeg binary
    
-   Call it via Rust:
    

```rust
Command::new("ffmpeg")
    .args(["-i", "input.heic", "output.png"])
    .output()?;
```

* * *

# 🚀 Bottom line

> ✅ Yes — FFmpeg can convert HEIC → PNG easily, quickly, and reliably  
> ⚡ It’s often the **simplest production-ready solution** in Rust/Tauri

* * *

-   Bundle FFmpeg properly in Tauri (cross-platform)
    
-   Or compare FFmpeg vs `libheif` for your exact use case
So if my objectives are audio, video, images even on the web api should I use ffmpeg vs sharp?

Short answer: **don’t choose one — use both, but for different jobs.**

> 🧠 **FFmpeg ≠ Sharp replacement**  
> They solve _different layers_ of the problem.

* * *

# 🎯 The clean architecture

## 🎬 Use **FFmpeg** for:

-   Video processing (transcode, resize, thumbnails)
    
-   Audio processing (convert, trim, normalize)
    
-   HEIC → PNG/JPEG
    
-   Extract frames from video
    
-   Anything involving codecs
    

* * *

## 🖼️ Use **Sharp** for:

-   Fast image resizing (thumbnails, avatars)
    
-   Cropping, compositing, overlays
    
-   Format conversion (JPEG/PNG/WebP/AVIF)
    
-   Image pipelines in your API
    

* * *

# ⚖️ Why you shouldn’t replace Sharp with FFmpeg

Even though FFmpeg _can_ process images:

### ❌ FFmpeg is not ideal for:

-   High-throughput image APIs
    
-   Complex image pipelines (resize + crop + overlay + mask)
    
-   Developer ergonomics
    

### ✅ Sharp is:

-   Built on **libvips** (extremely fast for images)
    
-   Designed for **batch image processing at scale**
    
-   Much simpler API for image tasks
    

* * *

# ⚡ Real-world comparison

| Task | Best tool |
| --- | --- |
| Resize 1000 images | Sharp 🔥 |
| Convert HEIC → PNG | FFmpeg ✅ |
| Generate video thumbnails | FFmpeg 🔥 |
| Crop + overlay watermark | Sharp 🔥 |
| Transcode video | FFmpeg 🔥 |
| Audio conversion | FFmpeg 🔥 |

* * *

# 🧭 For your stack (Tauri + Web API)

## Backend (API)

-   **Sharp** → image endpoints
    
-   **FFmpeg** → video/audio endpoints
    

## Tauri (Rust side)

-   **FFmpeg CLI** → everything media-related
    
-   Optional: `photon-rs` → lightweight image edits
    

* * *

# 🧠 Mental model

Think of it like this:

-   **Sharp** = _Photoshop automation for images_
    
-   **FFmpeg** = _media engine for everything (video/audio/images decoding)_
    

* * *

# 🔥 Recommended setup

### ✅ Best combo

-   Keep **Sharp** in your Node API
    
-   Use **FFmpeg** wherever media is involved
    

* * *

# ⚠️ When you _can_ drop Sharp

You could go FFmpeg-only if:

-   You don’t do heavy image transformations
    
-   Just basic convert/resize
    
-   Want a single binary dependency
    

👉 But you’ll lose:

-   Performance for image-heavy workloads
    
-   Clean developer experience
    

* * *

# 🚀 Bottom line

> 👉 **Use FFmpeg for media (audio/video/HEIC)**  
> 👉 **Use Sharp for images (fast, scalable pipelines)**

That combo is what most production systems end up with.

* * *

I’m not sure your advise is consistent. If ffmpeg solves the app in desktop (tauri) why not also use it on the API to keep a unified way of doing operations? It’s starting to look complex if I only use ffmpeg to HEIC TO PNG or HEIC to JPG. I have a unified ask that handles queuing and processing and the transport layer is the only thing that changes. In the web it uses the API’s via fetch, in Tauri it uses Rust. But so far Rust performance has been slow. So I’m considering trying different bindings. I would want to use a multithreaded one. Because I think that’s where I’m wasting the most time. It’s currently around 12 seconds per image (4MB) which is worrying.

You’re not wrong to want **one unified pipeline** — that’s actually a _good instinct_. The inconsistency you’re feeling comes from mixing **“best tool per task”** advice with **“system design simplicity”**.

Let’s straighten this out:

* * *

# 🧠 First: your 12 seconds is NOT normal

> 🚨 **12 seconds for a 4MB HEIC → PNG is a red flag**

Expected:

-   **FFmpeg (CLI):** ~100–300 ms
    
-   Even slow setups: <1 second
    

So before choosing architecture, we need to fix this.

* * *

# 🔍 Likely causes of your slowdown

## 1\. ❌ Using a slow HEIC decoder

If you’re using:

-   Pure Rust HEIC crates (many are immature)
    
-   WASM-based decoding
    
-   Incorrect bindings
    

👉 This is the **#1 likely issue**

* * *

## 2\. ❌ Not using multithreading

-   Some Rust image crates are **single-threaded**
    
-   FFmpeg uses **multi-threading by default**
    

* * *

## 3\. ❌ Debug build (huge one)

Rust debug builds are **orders of magnitude slower**

👉 Check:

```bash
cargo build --release
```

Running debug vs release can easily be:

> ⚠️ **10x–50x slower**

* * *

## 4\. ❌ Inefficient pipeline

-   Loading entire image multiple times
    
-   Converting formats unnecessarily
    
-   Blocking main thread
    

* * *

# 🧭 Now: your architecture question (the important part)

You said:

> “Why not just use FFmpeg everywhere for consistency?”

👉 **That’s actually a very reasonable strategy.**

* * *

# ✅ When a “FFmpeg-only” architecture makes sense

Given your setup:

-   Tauri (Rust)
    
-   Web API
    
-   Queue-based processing
    
-   Unified pipeline abstraction
    

👉 I would recommend:

> ✅ **Standardize on FFmpeg across everything**

* * *

# 🧱 Your system would look like:

## Shared pipeline logic (conceptual)

```text
Task → Queue → Worker → FFmpeg → Output
```

## Transport layer:

-   Web → API (Node or Rust)
    
-   Desktop → Tauri (Rust)
    

👉 Same commands, same behavior everywhere

* * *

# ⚖️ Tradeoff: FFmpeg vs Sharp (revisited correctly)

You’re optimizing for:

-   ✅ Consistency
    
-   ✅ Predictability
    
-   ✅ Shared logic
    
-   ✅ Queue processing
    

NOT:

-   Maximum image micro-optimizations
    

So:

| Choice | Result |
| --- | --- |
| FFmpeg-only | ✅ Simple, unified |
| Sharp + FFmpeg | ❌ Split logic |

👉 For _your architecture_, FFmpeg wins.

* * *

# ⚠️ The real issue: how you’re using FFmpeg in Rust

If Rust is slow, the problem is almost certainly:

> ❌ **Bindings / decoding layer — NOT FFmpeg itself**

* * *

# 🥇 What you should do instead

## Option 1 (BEST): Use FFmpeg CLI everywhere

In Rust (Tauri):

```rust
use std::process::Command;

Command::new("ffmpeg")
    .args([
        "-i", "input.heic",
        "-threads", "0", // auto
        "output.png"
    ])
    .output()?;
```

👉 This gives you:

-   Native FFmpeg performance
    
-   Multithreading
    
-   Zero binding overhead
    
-   Identical behavior to backend
    

* * *

## Option 2: `ffmpeg-next` (if you REALLY want Rust-native)

-   Still uses FFmpeg under the hood
    
-   More control
    
-   More complexity
    
-   Easy to get wrong → slow
    

👉 Only worth it if you need frame-level processing

* * *

# 🚀 Strong recommendation

Given everything you said:

> ✅ **Go all-in on FFmpeg (CLI) for both API and Tauri**

And fix performance by:

### ✅ Checklist

-   Build Rust in `--release`
    
-   Use FFmpeg CLI (not Rust image crates)
    
-   Ensure FFmpeg has `libheif`
    
-   Add `-threads 0`
    
-   Avoid WASM decoders
    

* * *

# 🧪 Sanity check (you should run this)

Run directly in terminal:

```bash
time ffmpeg -i input.heic output.png
```

If this is fast (<0.5s):  
👉 Your problem is your Rust pipeline

If this is slow:  
👉 Your FFmpeg build is broken

* * *

# 💡 Final takeaway

-   Your **architecture idea is solid**
    
-   Your **tool choice (FFmpeg everywhere) is valid**
    
-   Your **performance issue is implementation, not strategy**
    

* * *

> 🥇 **Sharp is usually 2×–5× faster than FFmpeg for image pipelines**

So for most apps:

> ✅ **The difference is noticeable in benchmarks, but negligible in UX**

-   Operations: resize + crop + convert → PNG/JPEG
    

* * *

| Tool | Avg time |

* * *

Resize + crop + convert:

| Tool | Avg total |
| --- | --- |
| Sharp | **50–150 ms** |
| FFmpeg | **150–400 ms** |

* * *

# 📊 What this actually means

## 🧠 In practice:

### For a single image:

-   Sharp: ~0.1s
    
-   FFmpeg: ~0.3s
    

👉 Difference: **~200 ms**

* * *

### For 10 images:

-   Sharp: ~1s
    
-   FFmpeg: ~2–3s
    

👉 Difference: noticeable but still reasonable

* * *

### For 1000 images (batch processing):

-   Sharp: ~1–2 min
    
-   FFmpeg: ~3–6 min
    

👉 Difference becomes **significant at scale**

* * *

# 🔥 Where Sharp really wins

Sharp dominates when:

-   High throughput APIs (thousands of images)
    
-   Memory efficiency matters
    
-   Complex pipelines (multiple transforms chained)
    

* * *

# 🤝 Where FFmpeg is “good enough”

FFmpeg is totally fine when:

-   You want **one unified system**
    
-   You already depend on it (video/audio)
    
-   Image workload is moderate
    
-   You value **consistency over max performance**
    

* * *

# 🚨 Important context for YOU

You said:

-   Queue-based processing
    
-   Same pipeline across Tauri + API
    
-   Already using FFmpeg
    
-   Currently seeing **12s per image (broken)**
    

👉 Once fixed, your reality will be:

> FFmpeg ≈ 150–300 ms  
> Sharp ≈ 50–150 ms

* * *

# 🧭 Decision framework

## ✅ Choose FFmpeg-only if:

-   You want **one system everywhere**
    
-   You already rely on FFmpeg heavily
    
-   You don’t process thousands of images per minute
    
-   Simplicity > raw speed
    

* * *

## ✅ Choose Sharp + FFmpeg if:

-   Image processing is your **core workload**
    
-   You need maximum throughput
    
-   You run large-scale image APIs
    

* * *

# 💡 My honest recommendation (based on your setup)

Given:

-   Tauri + Rust
    
-   Unified queue system
    
-   Desire for consistency
    
-   Current debugging complexity
    

👉 I would go:

> ✅ **FFmpeg everywhere**

Because:

-   The performance difference is **not worth the architectural split**
    
-   Your current bottleneck is **not FFmpeg**
    
-   You’ll gain **simplicity + maintainability**
    

* * *

```typescript
import { spawn } from 'child_process';

function ffmpegConvert(inputBuffer: Buffer, format: string): Promise<Buffer> {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const args = [
      '-i', 'pipe:0',
      '-f', format,
      'pipe:1'
    ];

    const ff = spawn('ffmpeg', args);

    const chunks: Buffer[] = [];

    ff.stdout.on('data', (chunk) => chunks.push(chunk));
    ff.stderr.on('data', () => {}); // ignore logs

    ff.on('close', (code) => {
      if (code === 0) {
        resolve(Buffer.concat(chunks));
      } else {
        reject(new Error(`FFmpeg exited with code ${code}`));
      }
    });

    ff.stdin.write(inputBuffer);
    ff.stdin.end();
  });
}
```

I'm using ffmpeg I think. my original .heic was 4.2 Mb and my converted PNG is 36.9MB is that expected?

Yes — **that is completely expected** 👍

* * *

# 🧠 Why your PNG exploded in size

You went from:

-   **4.2 MB (HEIC)** → highly compressed (HEVC)
    
-   **36.9 MB (PNG)** → lossless bitmap compression
    

👉 That jump is normal.

* * *

# 📦 What’s actually happening

## HEIC (your input)

-   Uses **HEVC compression** (same family as video codecs)
    
-   Extremely efficient
    
-   Throws away data (lossy)
    

👉 That’s why 12MP images can be ~2–5 MB

* * *

## PNG (your output)

-   **Lossless**
    
-   Stores near-raw pixel data + compression
    
-   Much less efficient for photos
    

👉 Especially bad for:

-   Photos
    
-   Gradients
    
-   High detail images
    

* * *

# 📊 Typical size comparison

| Format | Typical size (12MP photo) |
| --- | --- |
| HEIC | 2–5 MB |
| JPEG | 3–8 MB |
| PNG | **20–50 MB** |

👉 Your **36.9 MB** is right in the normal range

* * *

# ⚠️ Important takeaway

> ❗ PNG is usually the _wrong format_ for photos

* * *

# 🧭 What you should use instead

## 📸 For photos (HEIC input)

### ✅ JPEG (best default)

```bash
ffmpeg -i input.heic -q:v 2 output.jpg
```

-   ~3–6 MB
    
-   Great quality
    
-   Fast
    

* * *

### ✅ WebP (modern choice)

```bash
ffmpeg -i input.heic -c:v libwebp -quality 80 output.webp
```

-   Smaller than JPEG
    
-   Good quality
    

* * *

### ✅ AVIF (best compression)

```bash
ffmpeg -i input.heic -c:v libaom-av1 -crf 30 output.avif
```

-   Smallest size
    
-   Slower encode
    

* * *

## 🖼️ When PNG makes sense

Only use PNG if:

-   Transparency is needed
    
-   Logos / UI / graphics
    
-   Pixel-perfect output required
    

* * *

# ⚡ How to reduce PNG size (if you must use it)

FFmpeg example:

```bash
ffmpeg -i input.heic -compression_level 9 output.png
```

👉 But:

-   Won’t reduce it dramatically
    
-   PNG just isn’t efficient for photos
    

* * *

# 🧠 Why this matters for your system

If your pipeline defaults to PNG:

-   ❌ Bigger storage
    
-   ❌ Slower transfers
    
-   ❌ More memory usage
    

* * *

# 🚀 Recommendation for your app

Default strategy:

| Input | Output |
| --- | --- |
| HEIC photo | **JPEG or WebP** |
| UI/graphics | PNG |
